title: Health-Based Cyanotoxin Guideline Values Allow for Cyanotoxin-Based Monitoring and Efficient Public Health Response to Cyanobacterial Blooms
what: A peer-reviewed paper (Toxins, 2015, 7(2):457-477) in which the Oregon Health Authority (OHA) describes how, absent U.S. federal recreational-water criteria, it derived its own health-based guideline values (for drinking water, human recreational exposure, and dog recreational exposure) for four cyanotoxins — anatoxin-

claims.c: In the absence of U.S. federal cyanotoxin criteria for recreational water, OHA derived its own health-based guideline values for the four most common cyanotoxins in Oregon's fresh waters (anatoxin-a, cylindrospermopsin, microcystins, saxitoxins), with three separate values per toxin for drinking water, human recreational exposure, and dog recreational exposure.

Monitoring that uses cyanobacteria genus/species identification and cell enumeration as a surrogate for actual cyanotoxin presence can overestimate health risk and lead to unnecessary health advisories, because not all cyanobacteria produce toxins and toxin-producers do not always do so at harmful levels.

Guideline concentrations (µg/L) were derived from a toxicological Tolerable Daily Intake (TDI, µg/kg-day) using the formula Guideline Value = TDI x BW x RSC / IR, with drinking-water exposure assumed at 60 kg body weight and 2 L/day intake, and recreational exposure assumed at 20 kg body weight (representing a 4-6-year-old child) and 0.1 L/day intake, both with a Relative Source Contribution (RSC) of 1 (i.e., 100% of exposure assumed to occur via water).

For microcystin and saxitoxin, applying the formula with the derived human TDI (0.05 µg/kg-day for both) and the drinking-water exposure factors produced a calculated value of 1.5 µg/L, which OHA then rounded down to a final published guideline of 1.0 µg/L for extra public-health protection.

Between 2009 and 2014, Oregon issued 88 recreational HAB advisories in total; 78 were triggered by cyanobacteria cell counts exceeding a threshold, and only 10 were triggered by measured cyanotoxin concentrations exceeding a toxin guideline value.

TBM demonstrably averted at least 13 advisories over the study period — cases where cyanobacteria cell counts exceeded the advisory threshold but measured toxin concentrations stayed below the toxin guideline value — with additional advisories likely averted in individual years (12 in 2012, 14 in 2013, 13 in 2014) that OHA could not fully verify without complete cell-count data.

Adding toxin-based criteria alongside cell-count criteria did not meaningfully change how long the public was kept under advisory: mean advisory duration was 8.0 weeks for cell-count-based advisories versus 7.8 weeks for toxin-based advisories.
A 2009 incident in which dogs died along the South Umpqua River in Douglas County, Oregon was confirmed as cyanotoxin-related; the deceased dog's stomach contents contained 10 µg/L anatoxin-a.

Even during blooms severe enough that multiple cyanobacteria genera (Microcystis, Dolichospermum, Aphanizomenon, and in one case Oscillatoria) simultaneously exceeded cell-count advisory thresholds, measured total microcystin concentrations reached as high as 705 µg/L and 310 µg/L in two separate documented cases — tens to hundreds of times above the state's 10 µg/L human recreational guideline.

Despite high concentrations detected in raw/source water (peak of 5.24 µg/L microcystin-LW), no Oregon finished (treated) drinking water sample exceeded the drinking water guideline values during the study period; the highest cyanotoxin level ever detected in finished drinking water was 0.3 µg/L anatoxin-a.

The four-toxin guideline framework does not cover other cyanobacterial metabolites of concern, including BMAA, which is produced by most cyanobacteria and has been implicated by human epidemiological studies in elevated risk of neurodegenerative diseases such as ALS and Parkinson's disease, but for which no established dose-response relationship exists to allow a guideline to be set — leading the authors to caution that toxin-only monitoring programs could underestimate overall public health risk.

title: Repeated low-dose copper algaecide treatments promote beneficial phytoplankton and zooplankton communities
what: A 2025 peer-reviewed field study (Journal of Environmental Management) reporting a 42-day field mesocosm experiment in a hypereutrophic aquaculture pond that compares repeated, lower "MLR" (pH- and dissolved-organic-carbon-based) copper sulfate dosing against conventional, higher alkalinity-based copper dosing for cyan

claims.c: Conventional copper sulfate treatment using alkalinity-based dosing commonly prescribes higher copper doses than are actually necessary to achieve HAB control in many systems.

The experiment compared three copper dosing regimens — alkalinity-based (350 µg/L Cu), full MLR-based (80 µg/L Cu), and half-MLR (40 µg/L Cu) — each reapplied every 14 days for 3 applications across a 42-day field mesocosm trial in a hypereutrophic aquaculture pond, designed to mimic real repeated-application practice.

All three copper doses — including the two much lower MLR-based doses — produced an identical, strong initial reduction in cyanobacteria, indicating the lower doses controlled the bloom just as effectively at the outset as the higher conventional dose.
Over the full 42-day course of the experiment, the lower MLR-based doses caused less harm than the higher alkalinity-based dose to beneficial chlorophytes (green algae) and to zooplankton.
With repeated applications, the higher alkalinity-based dose became progressively less effective at controlling harmful phytoplankton, whereas the lower doses did not show this decline in efficacy.

The authors conclude that a repeated, low, MLR-based copper dosing strategy gives better sustained (long-term) HAB control with less ecological impact than the conventional higher, alkalinity-based dosing approach.

The MLR-based dosing approach tested here computes the copper dose from water pH and dissolved organic carbon (DOC) rather than from alkalinity, and was developed in prior work; this paper's contribution is testing that method under repeated, real-world-style dosing rather than introducing the method itself.

title: About the Nutrients Annex (Annex 4)
year: Undated EPA program webpage; most recent milestone referenced is DAP renewal "up for renewal in 2023"; targets discussed were adopted February 2016
what: An EPA program webpage explaining Annex 4 (Nutrients) of the binational U.S.-Canada Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA), under which both countries committed to Domestic Action Plans and formally adopted (Feb 2016) numeric phosphorus load-reduction targets and concentration objectives for Lake Erie to address e

claims.c: Lake Erie's nearshore waters are described as experiencing the most severe eutrophication among Great Lakes nearshore waters, with 67% rated in fair or poor condition in 2015.
Under Annex 4 of the Great Lakes Water Quality Agreement (GLWQA), the U.S. and Canada each committed to develop a Domestic Action Plan (DAP) for phosphorus reduction in their respective country.

In February 2016 the U.S. and Canada formally adopted a target of a 40% reduction in total phosphorus loads entering the western and central basins of Lake Erie, intended to bring the central basin to an annual load of 6,000 metric tons.

A parallel 40% reduction target was set for spring total and soluble reactive phosphorus (SRP) loads specifically from the Maumee River (US), plus matching 40% targets for other priority tributaries: Thames River and Leamington-area tributaries in Canada, and River Raisin, Portage River, Toussaint Creek, Sandusky River, and the Huron River (Ohio) in the US.
No numeric phosphorus load-reduction target was set for Lake Erie's eastern basin, because the nuisance-algae (Cladophora) problem there is attributed to multiple factors beyond phosphorus loading.

Annex 4 set basin-specific total phosphorus concentration objectives for Lake Erie: western basin interim objective 15 µg/L (expected to reach 12 µg/L once load reductions are implemented); central basin interim objective 10 µg/L (expected 6 µg/L); eastern basin interim objective 10 µg/L (expected 6 µg/L).

The phosphorus load and concentration targets were derived using nine different computer simulation models relating phosphorus loading levels to algal growth outcomes, referenced against 2008 as the baseline year, chosen for its data quality and because it was considered a representative 'average' year for Lake Erie conditions.

Implementation has proceeded on a defined timeline: the GLWQA (which contains Annex 4) was renewed in 2012; a target review/evaluation process began around 2013; the phosphorus targets were formally adopted in February 2016; the first five-year Domestic Action Plans were issued in 2018 and were up for renewal in 2023; and the binational Lake Erie Phosphorus Reduction Strategy was published in 2019.

The Annex 4 targets are meant to address three distinct eutrophication-related problems that differ by Lake Erie basin: western basin cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) blooms and associated toxins, central basin seasonal hypoxia (low dissolved oxygen), and eastern basin nuisance growth of the alga Cladophora.

The 2019 Lake Erie Binational Phosphorus Reduction Strategy is structured around four components: an updated environmental-condition assessment for lake-wide nutrient management, a summary of the target-development process and load allocation between the two countries, binational priorities for phosphorus management (including watershed prioritization and research/monitoring priorities), and a progress-tracking methodology using an adaptive-management approach.

The source states an explicit ecological trade-off limitation on the phosphorus-reduction strategy: reducing phosphorus too far is itself framed as a risk, since insufficient nutrients could threaten fisheries.

title: CAPTAIN LIQUID Pesticide Product Label
what: An EPA-accepted FIFRA registration-review decision letter and stamped product label (dated March 23, 2022) for CAPTAIN® LIQUID COPPER ALGAECIDE (EPA Reg. No. 67690-9, Decision No. 559224), a copper-ethanolamine-complex algaecide/herbicide made by SePRO Corporation, labeled for controlling algae (including cyanobacteria

claims.c: Captain is a chelated copper (copper ethanolamine complex) formulation labeled as effective against a broad range of green algae and cyanobacteria (blue-green algae) — filamentous, planktonic, and macrophytic forms — and as an herbicide on copper-susceptible submersed weeds.
The active ingredient is a copper ethanolamine complex at 28.2% concentration, equivalent to 9.1% metallic copper.
The label imposes a blanket ceiling of 1.0 ppm metallic copper for any single application to any water, with an effective-control range of 0.2-1.0 ppm.

Copper does not break down in the environment (unlike most organic pesticides) and therefore accumulates in treated waters with repeated applications, which the label ties to its toxicity to fish and aquatic organisms.

Killing algae/plant biomass with this product can itself deplete dissolved oxygen as the dead biomass decomposes, risking fish and invertebrate suffocation; the label's mitigation is to cap any single pass at half the water body and require at least 14 days between treatments.

Applying algaecide to high-density cyanobacterial blooms can rupture cells and release intracellular toxins (hepatotoxins and neurotoxins) into the water, so the label directs applicators to avoid treating high-density toxic-cyanobacteria blooms rather than to use treatment as a mitigation for them.

The label sets explicit water-chemistry thresholds below which non-target acute toxicity risk (e.g., to koi and trout) increases: pH ≤ 6.5, dissolved organic carbon (DOC) ≤ 3.0 mg/L, and alkalinity < 50 mg/L ("soft" water); the approved rates assume water above all three thresholds.

For whole-waterbody treatment, the label caps cumulative annual dosing at 21.9 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot per year, built from up to 8 applications/year at 1 ppm each, staggering treatment of each half of the water body every 14 days across an 8-month (244-day) season.

For partial-waterbody treatment (discrete sections summing to no more than half the waterbody within the 14-day retreatment window), the annual cap roughly doubles to 46.6 lbs metallic copper per acre-foot per year, from up to 17 applications/year.

For aquaculture ponds with fish present, the label imposes a stricter single-application ceiling of 0.4 ppm metallic copper when targeting nuisance algae, below the general 1.0 ppm cap used elsewhere.
Treatment with Captain does not by itself make water potable; drinking-water sources require additional, separate potable-water treatment after algaecide application.

claims.c: This 2009 document is a formal amendment to the original Coppers RED (completed July 2006, published August 2006), issued after a public comment period that drew comments from growers, university extension services, registrants, and publicly-owned treatment works.

EPA conducted a qualitative human-health assessment for copper, not a quantitative dose-response assessment, because available data show copper is efficiently regulated by the human body with no identified systemic toxicity.

EPA's screening-level ecological risk assessment found that copper pesticides can pose acute risk to non-target organisms, with aquatic organisms facing the greatest documented risk, attributed to direct water application and to runoff from adjacent treated fields.

This RED's ecological risk assessment covers only agricultural uses of copper; a separate ecological assessment of antimicrobial uses (e.g., pools, spas, fountains, urban runoff/sewer discharge, anti-fouling coatings, wood preservatives) was still pending at the time of this document.

EPA regulates a direct-water 'Algae Control' use of copper products (Appendix A use site) capped at a maximum concentration of 0.4 parts per million (ppm) per application; the 2009 amendment narrows this limit to apply specifically in aquaculture ponds when fish are present.

Because many current copper product labels lack specified application rates or retreatment intervals, EPA's ecological exposure assessment used conservative benchmark rates well above real label rates: the highest single rate actually allowed on labels is 8.0 lbs Cu2+ (metallic copper) per acre (dormant fruit/nut tree application), versus a highest assessed benchmark of 31.8 lbs Cu2+/acre used in the ecological assessment (applications to filberts).

Despite program activity, Lake Erie continues to show eutrophic conditions, major tributaries routinely exceed target phosphorus levels, and basin-wide load reductions are difficult to attribute to management because loads and in-lake responses are dominated by year-to-year precipitation/discharge variability.

title: U.S. Action Plan for Lake Erie 2018-2023 (Commitments and Strategy for Phosphorus Reduction)
what: A binational U.S.–Canada policy action plan (finalized February 2018, covering 2018-2023), developed by USEPA's Great Lakes National Program Office with five U.S. states and multiple federal agencies, that lays out phosphorus-load reduction commitments and targets for Lake Erie under Annex 4 (Nutrients) of the 2012 Gre

claims.c: Lake Erie is a drinking-water source for 12 million people in the U.S. and Canada, and harmful/nuisance algal growth has risen sharply over the past decade, linked to high phosphorus delivery from major rivers during spring storms, threatening a $12.9 billion tourism industry.
During the 2011 Lake Erie bloom, measured microcystin concentrations reached 50x the WHO safe body-contact limit and 1,200x the WHO safe drinking-water limit.
In August 2014, more than 500,000 people lost drinking water for three days when algal toxins forced closure of the Toledo, Ohio drinking-water treatment plant.

Lake Erie's Central Basin hypoxic ('dead zone') area has averaged about 4,500 km2 since the early 2000s, with the largest single event of 8,800 km2 in 2012 (the year after the record 2011 bloom); 2015 is cited as the worst bloom year on record as of this report.
In 2012, hypoxic conditions were linked to a large fish kill — tens of thousands of dead fish washing up along roughly a 40 km stretch of Ontario shoreline.

Phosphorus load is unevenly distributed across Lake Erie's three basins: Western 61%, Central 28%, Eastern 11% of the whole-lake annual total-phosphorus load, correlating with different basin depths and algal problems.
The Western Basin, being shallow, warm, and phosphorus-rich (fed by the Detroit and Maumee Rivers), regularly hosts blooms of the toxin-producing cyanobacterium Microcystis aeruginosa.

The Eastern Basin, despite much lower phosphorus levels than the Western/Central basins, supports excessive growth of the filamentous green alga Cladophora on rocky substrate, causing beach fouling, odor, and clogged intakes rather than cyanotoxin risk.

The plan cites at least three different population figures for Lake Erie's human footprint in different contexts: 12 million (binational drinking-water users), 'more than 10 million' (basin residents), and 'over 11 million' (population served, in a cross-Great-Lakes comparison) — figures that should not be treated as interchangeable.

Since the spread of invasive zebra and quagga mussels (established in Lake Erie in the 1990s), nearshore nutrient recycling has increased via the mussels' filtering/excretion, and greater water clarity has let algae grow at greater depths — factors the plan links to increased nearshore nuisance algal growth, alongside wetland/riparian loss and more intense spring storms.

'Dissolved phosphorus' (also called soluble/dissolved reactive phosphorus) is more readily taken up by algae than particulate phosphorus, and its share of Lake Erie's total P load has grown since the resurgence of blooms in the late 1990s.

The plan's strategy targets phosphorus alone and assumes P-focused actions will also reduce nitrogen, yet it cites Gobler et al. (2016) for the finding that phosphorus reduction without nitrogen reduction would not reduce bloom toxicity (blooms would shrink in extent but could remain toxic, possibly longer).
Under the 2012 GLWQA, the U.S. and Canada adopted six binational 'Lake Ecosystem Objectives,' including explicitly capping cyanobacterial toxin levels judged to threaten human or ecosystem health.

Central-basin hypoxia target: a 40% reduction in total phosphorus (TP) entering the western and central basins (vs. a 2008 baseline) to reach an annual central-basin load of 6,000 metric tons, split as a 3,316 metric-ton reduction from the U.S. and 212 metric tons from Canada.

Nearshore algal-species target: a 40% reduction in spring TP and soluble reactive phosphorus (SRP) loads from named priority watersheds (Canada: Thames River, Leamington tributaries; U.S.: Maumee, River Raisin, Portage, Toussaint Creek, Sandusky, and Huron [Ohio] rivers).

Western-basin cyanotoxin target: a 40% reduction in spring (March-July) TP/SRP loads from the Maumee River specifically, equating (vs. the 2008 baseline) to a spring load target of 860 metric tons TP and 186 metric tons SRP.

The plan states the science is unclear whether phosphorus-load reductions from Eastern-basin sources are warranted for Cladophora control, so it defers firm Eastern-basin targets and commits only to precautionary actions and research, with a re-evaluation planned for 2020.

Most of Lake Erie's annual phosphorus load comes from a handful of tributaries (Maumee, Detroit, Sandusky in the U.S.; Thames, Grand in Ontario); nonpoint-source runoff is estimated to supply about 72% of the total annual P load lake-wide, and up to about 89% of the annual TP load in the western-basin's tributaries.

The plan states Maumee River phosphorus loads are 'the single best predictor' of western-basin bloom severity (attributed to its high P concentration), whereas the higher-flow Detroit River (95% Lake Huron-derived flow) has a much lower TP concentration (0.014 mg/L vs. Maumee's 0.42 mg/L, 2011-2013) that the plan says is 'too low to spur an algal bloom' even though its cumulative load still contributes to downstream algal growth and hypoxia.

title: USDA Legacy Phosphorus Assessment Project
year: Project ongoing (component project duration March 2020-September 2025); flagship synthesis paper published online September 29, 2024, issue dated July-August 2025
what: A multi-year USDA NRCS-ARS interagency research initiative, run under the Conservation Effects Assessment Project (CEAP), that samples soils and stream/ditch sediments across seven U.S. agricultural watersheds (in the Chesapeake Bay, Lake Champlain, Lake Erie, Mississippi River, and Snake River basins) to characterize

claims.c: The project's premise is that phosphorus accumulated in agricultural soils over decades ('legacy P') continues to threaten water quality long after inputs are reduced, framing the need for management focused on existing soil/sediment P stocks rather than only current fertilizer inputs.
Measured stocks of labile (readily exchangeable) phosphorus in surface soils/sediments were large enough, at all seven study sites, to sustain high dissolved P losses for decades without new inputs.

All seven sites contained surface soils capable of sustaining dissolved P losses above a reference high-loss benchmark (the 75th percentile of measured field dissolved-P loss, 0.5 kg P/ha) for decades or longer, and most surface sediments held more than 1 kg P/ha in labile form.
Topsoil (0-5 cm) is enriched in labile P relative to subsoil (5-15 cm) at most sites, and labile P is the most vertically stratified P pool measured, more so than Mehlich-3 P, Olsen P, or total P.

A single across-site statistical model (a 'quantity-intensity-capacity' Generalized Additive Model predicting EPC0, the equilibrium phosphate concentration at net-zero sorption, from labile P and buffering variables) explained the large majority of variation in EPC0 and generalized better than fitting separate models per site.

Sediments from stream/ditch channels differ systematically from adjacent field soils in P sorption behavior (sediments generally showed higher sorption capacity and lower equilibrium phosphate concentration, EPC0, than soils), so the two matrices are not interchangeable when assessing legacy P risk.

Labile-P concentrations measured at these seven agriculturally-influenced sites are well above levels reported for soils without anthropogenic P enrichment, consistent with (but not proof of) legacy buildup from historical agricultural P inputs.

The study's sampling design was not built to formally test the effects of land use or tillage management on P lability, so associations between management history and measured P properties are descriptive and site-specific rather than statistically confirmed drivers.

A related component of the broader effort, an ARS project focused specifically on the Western Lake Erie Basin, uses an edge-of-field dataset from 46 agricultural fields plus laboratory desorption experiments and phosphate isotope tracing to distinguish legacy P from recently-applied P and to inform P-management recommendations for that basin.

The overarching project pairs edge-of-field monitoring (coordinated by NRCS) with in-stream and watershed-outlet (HUC12) monitoring (coordinated by other partners) across the five basins, to track how legacy phosphorus export behaves over time at multiple spatial scales, feeding into improved P-management recommendations.

EPA's 10-day drinking water Health Advisory for cylindrospermopsin is 0.7 µg/L for bottle-fed infants and pre-school children (≤6 years) and 3.0 µg/L for school-age children through adults.
Both sets of advisory concentrations are explicitly tied to a 10-day exposure duration, i.e., they are short-term/acute guidance values, not chronic or lifetime health-based values.
EPA explicitly frames these Health Advisories as non-enforceable technical guidance, not a legally binding federal drinking-water regulation or standard.

Of the three cyanotoxins for which EPA produced a Health Effects Support Document (anatoxin-a, cylindrospermopsin, microcystins), only two (cylindrospermopsin and microcystins) received a quantitative numeric drinking-water Health Advisory; no numeric HA value for anatoxin-a appears anywhere in the fetched material.

The Health Effects Support Documents (HESDs) underlying the advisories are literature-review compilations — covering chemical/physical properties, toxin synthesis and environmental fate, occurrence and exposure, and health effects — rather than new original experiments conducted by EPA.

EPA characterizes the Health Advisory values as provisional/revisable rather than fixed, stating they may change as new science emerges; the underlying documents nonetheless remain dated 2015 in the fetched material, with no revision evident.
The federal Health Advisory does not preempt states, which may set their own, potentially different, standards or guidelines for cyanotoxins in drinking water.

title: Freshwater Harmful Algal Blooms: Causes, Challenges, and Policy Considerations
what: A U.S. Congressional Research Service (CRS) report (No. R44871, last updated August 20, 2018) that surveys the causes and impacts of freshwater harmful algal blooms (HABs), summarizes the federal/state legislative and regulatory response (HABHRCA, Clean Water Act provisions, agency roles, funding), and lays out the fie

claims.c: CRS states that in recent years the frequency and geographic distribution of freshwater HABs have been increasing nationally and globally, with HABs recorded in every state.
The 2014 Lake Erie cyanobacterial HAB contaminated Toledo, Ohio's drinking water supply, affecting more than 500,000 people, with an estimated $65 million in lost benefits.
Between January 1 and August 12, 2016, U.S. states reported at least 266 notices (cautions, warnings, public health advisories/warnings) for freshwater HABs.

In EPA's 2012 National Lakes Assessment, microcystin was detected in 39% of lakes sampled — a 9.5-percentage-point increase from the 2007 assessment — while median lake total phosphorus rose from 20 µg/L (2007) to 37 µg/L (2012).

EPA's draft (non-binding) water quality criteria recommend numeric values of 4 µg/L for microcystin and 8 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin, while WHO's 2003 recreational-water guidance places microcystin exposure bands from <10 µg/L (low probability of adverse effects) up to >2,000 µg/L (very high probability).

As of November 2015, roughly 30 states had implemented their own cyanobacterial HAB guidelines for recreational waterways, of which 21 set numeric guidelines for microcystin or cylindrospermopsin (ranging 0.8-20 µg/L for microcystin and 4-20 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin) — i.e., no single federally mandated numeric threshold exists and state guidance varies widely.

Nutrient enrichment (nitrogen and phosphorus) is widely considered by scientists to be a key cause of HAB formation, but the Clean Water Act does not give EPA authority to directly regulate the nonpoint (diffuse, e.g., agricultural runoff) nutrient sources that are a major contributor, only point-source discharges.
The report states outright scientific/predictive uncertainty: it is not known precisely what nutrient level triggers a bloom, nor what factors trigger toxin release within a bloom.

Federal legislative authority for HABs (HABHRCA) has been amended three times — 1998 (P.L. 105-383), 2004 (P.L. 108-456), and 2014 (P.L. 113-124) — progressively expanding scope from marine-only to include freshwater HABs, adding CDC to the interagency task force, and authorizing $20,500,000 per year for FY2014-FY2018.

The President's FY2019 budget request proposed eliminating or drastically cutting numerous EPA nutrient/HAB-relevant grant programs (e.g., CWA §319 nonpoint-source grants and several EPA geographic programs cut to $0.0M; Great Lakes Restoration cut from $300.0M enacted to $30.0M requested), which the report says creates uncertainty about whether EPA can still meet its nutrient-reduction goals.

Golden algae (a non-cyanobacterial freshwater HAB taxon) are described as an emerging problem that has caused large fish kills worldwide, including millions of fish in Texas, with most major fish kills occurring since 2000; per two affected states, there is currently no evidence golden algal toxins directly threaten humans, other mammals, or birds.

Under CWA §303(d), 24 states have listed a combined 1,015 waters as impaired for algal blooms, while only 2 states (California and New Hampshire) have listed waters impaired specifically for algal toxins (67 waters total, plus only 2 TMDLs developed for algal toxins) — showing formal impairment listing for toxins specifically lags listing for blooms generally.

title: Water Treatment Optimization for Cyanotoxins (Version 1.0)
what: A U.S. EPA Office of Water technical guidance document (EPA 810-B-16-007, Version 1.0, October 2016) that gives drinking-water treatment plant managers and operators a framework — source-water monitoring indicators, cyanotoxin measurement methods, and a decision-tree distinguishing intracellular vs. extracellular cyano

claims.c: Warmer water temperatures (typically ≥25°C/77°F) favor growth of some cyanobacteria, while Microcystis growth is usually limited below 15°C (59°F); other genera such as Planktothrix and Cylindrospermopsis can survive in colder water, even under ice.

Turbidity, a common jar-testing optimization parameter for particulate removal, may not be a reliable indicator of cyanobacteria cell or toxin removal under certain conditions, particularly when raw water turbidity is below 10 NTU.

Wind can concentrate cyanobacteria cells into surface scums representing a thousand-fold to million-fold increase in cell concentration, which is especially concerning if this occurs near a public water system intake; strong winds can also mix surface blooms downward toward intake depths.

The presence or abundance of cyanobacteria cells does not reliably indicate whether toxins are present at a given time: toxins can occur even at low cell concentrations, and elevated toxin concentrations can persist after cells/blooms are no longer detectable in the source water.

Cyanotoxins can occur intracellularly (cell-bound) or extracellularly (dissolved); applying certain oxidants/disinfectants can lyse cells and release toxin, increasing the extracellular (dissolved) toxin concentration, which makes the choice and location of oxidant application a treatment challenge.

In June 2015 EPA issued drinking-water Health Advisories for only two cyanotoxins (total microcystins and cylindrospermopsin) and Health Effects Support Documents for three (adding anatoxin-a), because at that time there was insufficient information to set a Health Advisory specifically for anatoxin-a.

As of this document's writing, no specific, generally accepted in-plant treatment targets/goals existed for cyanobacteria or cyanotoxins; the document presents itself as only a step toward establishing such optimization goals.

The document defines 'treatment optimization' as achieving the best possible performance from each water-treatment unit process through process control and problem-solving (priority setting, tailored studies, data trending), continuously assessed against pre-established goals, in line with EPA's Composite Correction Program (CCP) 'multiple barrier' concept.

At the time of writing, there was no published EPA analytical method for phycocyanin (unlike chlorophyll-a, which has EPA Method 445.0), so phycocyanin measurement relied on commercial in-vivo fluorescence probes/instruments rather than a standardized EPA method.

Indirect indicators such as visual inspection, turbidity, Secchi depth, and diurnal pH shifts can flag a possible cyanobacteria problem, but the document states these indicators do not substitute for quantitative, confirmatory cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin measurements.

The document organizes extracellular cyanotoxin treatment guidance around four technology categories — carbon adsorption, membranes, biofiltration, and oxidation — and supplies comparative tables (general oxidant effectiveness, and CT [concentration x time] tables for microcystin-LR and cylindrospermopsin) plus technology-specific process-evaluation appendices for both intracellular and extracellular toxin treatment.

title: State & Tribal Toxin Thresholds and HAB Advisory Decision Methods | US EPA
year: 2026 (living/maintained webpage; page states "Last updated on June 25, 2026," no fixed publication year)
what: An EPA webpage that compiles, state by state and tribe by tribe, the numeric cyanotoxin thresholds (for anatoxin-a, microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, and saxitoxins, all in µg/L) that U.S. state and tribal programs actually use to trigger drinking-water and recreational-water HAB advisories, with a link to each jurisdi

claims.c: The page's own stated purpose is to compile the cyanotoxin thresholds that state and tribal programs use to make advisory decisions for drinking water and recreational uses.
The compilation is explicitly incomplete by design: it only lists thresholds for which EPA found publicly accessible documentation, with a source link given for each entry.

A large share of 'state' thresholds are not independently derived — many states simply adopted EPA's own national Health Advisory (HA) values for drinking water or Ambient Water Quality Criteria (AWQC) for recreational water as their action levels.

EPA's own baseline drinking-water Health Advisory values, which numerous states reference directly, are 0.3 µg/L microcystin for children under 6 and 1.6 µg/L for school-age children through adults, and 0.7 µg/L cylindrospermopsin for children under 6 and 3 µg/L for the general population.
EPA's recommended recreational-water criteria (AWQC), used as the default reference by many states, are 8 µg/L for microcystins and 15 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin.

Where states do set independent recreational microcystin advisory levels, the numeric trigger points vary roughly an order of magnitude around EPA's 8 µg/L reference, and the number/structure of tiers also differs — e.g., California starts a 'caution' tier at 1 µg/L and reaches 'danger' at 6 µg/L, while Nevada and Kansas keep a single wide 'warning' tier spanning roughly 8 µg/L up to 2,000 µg/L before a top-tier 'danger/hazard' designation.

Several states set materially lower cyanotoxin action levels for dogs than for humans at the same recreational waters — e.g., Oregon's dog-specific anatoxin-a level (0.4 µg/L) versus its 15 µg/L human recreational level, and Indiana's dog advisory/prohibited microcystin levels (0.4/0.8 µg/L) versus its human advisory/prohibited levels (8/20 µg/L).
The compiled framework covers all 50 U.S. states plus six tribal nations, each entry linking out to that jurisdiction's own underlying guidance document.

Across jurisdictions, advisory frameworks use inconsistent tier vocabularies and criteria types — plain detect/non-detect, caution/advisory, warning, danger/prohibition — and inconsistent stratification (age band, animal species, or shoreline vs. open water), rather than one harmonized national decision schema.

The page itself provides no on-page definition of EPA HA/AWQC methodology and no explicit caveat about the currency, verification, or accuracy of the compiled state values — a user must follow the outbound links to each state's or EPA's own source document for that detail.

title: U.S. Action Plan for Lake Erie: Commitments and Strategy for Phosphorus Reduction (August 2017 Draft)
what: A 2017 draft joint U.S. federal-state action plan (EPA's Great Lakes National Program Office with five Great Lakes states and multiple federal agencies) presenting phosphorus load-reduction commitments and management strategies for Lake Erie. It documents state- and federal-led actions implementing the binational nutri

claims.c: Lake Erie HABs pose acute drinking-water and public-health risk, illustrated by the August 2014 Toledo, Ohio water crisis and by extreme microcystin levels during the 2011 bloom relative to WHO safety limits.

Binational GLWQA Annex 4 phosphorus targets (adopted Feb. 2016, 2008 baseline) call for a 40% cut in total phosphorus loading to the western and central basins, equating to an annual central-basin load target of 6,000 metric tons, split as a 3,316-metric-ton reduction from the U.S. and a 212-metric-ton reduction from Canada.

A stricter, separate target applies to the Maumee River specifically: a 40% cut in spring (March–July) total and dissolved reactive phosphorus (DRP), equal to a 2008-baseline spring load of 860 metric tons TP and 186 metric tons DRP, intended to keep western-basin cyanobacteria below toxin-threat levels.

Lake Erie's three basins differ sharply in depth and phosphorus loading share, which the plan links to different bloom types: the shallow Western Basin gets ~61% of the lake's total phosphorus load and hosts toxin-producing Microcystis aeruginosa; the Central Basin gets 28% and experiences bottom-water hypoxia; the deep Eastern Basin gets only 11% but supports nuisance benthic Cladophora growth.

Central-basin hypoxia has grown since the early 2000s, averaging about 4,500 km² with a peak of 8,800 km² in 2012 (the year after the record 2011 bloom), and a 2012 low-oxygen event coincided with a large fish kill along 40 km of Ontario shoreline.

The plan's strategy targets phosphorus specifically (treated as the growth-limiting nutrient) and explicitly flags, via a cited study, that phosphorus reduction alone may not reduce bloom toxicity even if it shrinks bloom extent.

Whether phosphorus-load reductions are warranted in Lake Erie's Eastern Basin (where the main nuisance is benthic Cladophora rather than planktonic cyanobacteria) is explicitly unresolved; the U.S. and Canada deferred setting Eastern Basin targets and committed only to re-evaluate the question in 2020.

The plan attributes the post-1990s bloom resurgence to several concurrent, non-quantified factors: a shift toward more bioavailable dissolved (vs. particulate) phosphorus, invasive zebra/quagga mussels recycling nutrients nearshore, greater water clarity extending algal growth to depth, wetland/riparian loss, warming (longer growing seasons), and more intense spring storms delivering nutrients at critical times.

The plan's practical management levers are organized around a 10-point joint action list (Great Lakes Commission LENT working group, endorsed 2015), spanning agricultural nutrient-application restrictions, 4R nutrient stewardship certification, municipal discharger phosphorus cuts, green infrastructure, a 5-year phase-out of residential phosphorus fertilizer, and an explicit adaptive-management commitment to revisit the targets themselves.

title: Watershed Protection: Clean Lakes Case Study: Phosphorus Inactivation and Wetland Manipulation Improve Kezar Lake, NH
what: A U.S. EPA Clean Lakes Program case study (EPA 841-F-95-002, EPA Office of Water, published circa 1995) describing a New Hampshire Department of Environmental Services-led restoration of Kezar Lake, NH, that combined an in-lake aluminum-salt (alum) phosphorus-inactivation treatment with upstream Chadwick Meadows wetlan

claims.c: Eliminating most of the external (point-source) phosphorus load did not stop Kezar Lake's algal blooms, implicating internal (sediment) phosphorus loading as the sustaining cause and the rationale for an alum-based, sediment-targeting treatment.
Kezar Lake is a small, shallow, dimictic, phosphorus-limited lake with a well-defined morphometry used to frame the whole restoration analysis.

The main 1984 alum (aluminum sulfate + sodium aluminate) treatment covered 48 hectares at 40 g Al/m3, cost $65,604 total ($1,367/ha; 0.50 person-days/ha), applied by a custom-modified weed harvester rather than a barge, and was markedly cheaper per hectare and per labor-day than the barge-based Annabessacook Lake, ME comparator.
A separate, smaller pilot-scale aluminum application treated only 10 hectares of Kezar Lake, at a dose recorded in the source text as far lower/differently-united than the main 48-ha treatment.

Copper sulfate algicide was applied before the aluminum treatment specifically to release phosphorus that was bound up in phytoplankton, and a weed harvester was mechanically modified to apply the aluminum salts instead of using a barge.

Raising water levels at the 20-hectare Chadwick Meadows wetland removed more than 80% of incoming phosphorus during the summer growing season, but the wetland's overall, year-round effectiveness at removing phosphorus from Lion Brook was poor, and it could act as a phosphorus source rather than a sink during high-flow fall/spring periods.

Water-quality improvements from the 1984 treatment were not permanent: most monitored parameters showed their strongest response for roughly 2-4 years before drifting back toward pre-treatment conditions, though total phosphorus and transparency remained somewhat improved beyond that window and dissolved aluminum showed no impact at all.

The source proposes four specific, hedged (not confirmed) hypotheses for why treated water-quality parameters drifted back toward pre-treatment conditions: saturation of aluminum phosphorus-binding sites, downward migration of the aluminum floc through the sediment, continued tributary phosphorus inputs, and hypolimnetic anoxia re-releasing sediment phosphorus.
No water-column impact from dissolved aluminum was detected as a result of the treatment.

The treatment period coincided with a shift in zooplankton community composition (fewer cladocerans, loss of Daphnia as co-dominant, more Keratella, fewer Polyartha, more ciliates) that the source associates with altered food-web structure following the phytoplankton shift, and this altered community had not reverted as of 1988.

Later monitoring (through 1994) suggests year-to-year water-quality variability at Kezar Lake tracked annual precipitation, with the wet years 1988 and 1993 showing degraded conditions and the 1995 drought showing the best recorded transparency and lowest productivity - a confound the source uses to qualify how much of the water-quality trend can be attributed to the treatment itself.
Recreational use of the lake increased after restoration, with attendance at the adjoining Wadleigh State Park rising by roughly 2,000 visitors per summer in 1984 and 1986.

The project was executed and funded under the EPA Clean Lakes Program (Section 314 of the Clean Water Act), structured as a Phase I diagnostic/feasibility study beginning in 1980 followed by a Phase II restoration/protection project beginning in 1984.

title: Copper Algaecides — HCB-1 (Strategies for Preventing and Managing Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms)
what: A section of ITRC's web-based guidance document HCB-1 ("Strategies for Preventing and Managing Harmful Cyanobacterial Blooms"), published 2021, that describes copper-based algaecides (copper sulfate, acidified copper products, and chelated products such as copper ethanolamine, copper citrate, and copper gluconate) as a

claims.c: Copper algaecides have a long history of use against algae and cyanobacteria and have been extensively studied in the peer-reviewed literature covering efficacy, environmental fate of copper, and off-target aquatic life impacts.

Copper algaecides are typically deployed as a reactive/intervention measure once cyanobacteria reach high cell densities, begin producing cyanotoxins, or produce taste-and-odor compounds that interfere with water use.

Successful, low-risk use of copper algaecides depends on treatment timing, and sites with recurring HCB problems benefit from a documented management plan with explicit monitoring triggers (cell density, cyanotoxin concentration, or taste/odor compound concentration) rather than ad hoc treatment decisions.

Copper algaecide products (copper sulfate, acidified copper, and chelated forms including copper ethanolamine, copper citrate, and copper gluconate) are registered with the U.S. EPA for algae/cyanobacteria control, and specific application methods for lakes, reservoirs, ponds, and irrigation canals are governed by the individual product's EPA-approved label.

The degree and outcome of cyanobacterial response to copper algaecide treatment is not uniform — it varies with the target cyanobacteria's innate copper sensitivity and abundance, exposure duration, and site-specific water characteristics including hardness, alkalinity, conductivity, and pH.

Killing cyanobacterial cells with copper algaecide (or any algaecide) can lyse the cells and release previously intracellular cyanotoxins into the water column as dissolved toxin, creating a potential exposure risk distinct from the risk of the intact bloom.

Copper treatments can cause dissolved oxygen to decline as a secondary effect of decomposition of the algae/cyanobacteria killed by treatment, and this is a distinct operational risk from the toxin-release risk.

The guidance recommends staging copper algaecide applications (i.e., not treating an entire bloom/waterbody in a single pass) to avoid or manage the risk of significant post-treatment dissolved-oxygen decline.

General best-practice guidance in the HCB-1 document (applicable to algaecides and coagulation compounds generally, including copper products) is to use the minimum effective dose and to treat preferably during early stages of bloom development rather than after a bloom is already dense.

Dissolved organic carbon (DOC), pH, alkalinity, and hardness are treated as toxicity-modifying factors (TMFs) that govern how much free/bioavailable copper is present, and therefore how toxic a given nominal copper dose is to target and non-target organisms in a given waterbody.

tensions: This ITRC guidance treats copper algaecide cell-lysis and dissolved-oxygen decline as manageable, staging-mitigable operational risks within a broader "monitor, trigger, treat" management framework — a more permissive, use-it-carefully stance than sources that emphasize copper accumulation in sediment or non-target ecotoxicity as reasons to avoid or minimize copper treatment altogether. It also implicitly frames copper algaecide as a late-stage reactive intervention (triggered once cyanotoxins, cell density, or taste/odor already exceed a thres

title: Cyanobacterial toxins: microcystins — Background document for development of WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and Guidelines for safe recreational water environments (WHO/HEP/ECH/WSH/2020.6)
what: A 2020 WHO background document that reviews toxicokinetic, human, and animal toxicological evidence on microcystins (a family of cyanobacterial cyclic-peptide hepatotoxins) to derive provisional health-based guideline values for microcystins in drinking-water and recreational water, replacing WHO's 2003 microcystin-LR-

claims.c: WHO's provisional guideline values (GVs) for microcystins are 1 µg/L (lifetime drinking-water), 12 µg/L (short-term drinking-water) and 24 µg/L (recreational water), derived from a mouse study of MC-LR corroborated by a similar rat study, with the liver identified as the most sensitive organ.

The short-term drinking-water GV is meant only to bound temporary exceedances (up to 2 weeks) while treatment is upgraded, not repeated seasonal exceedance of the lifetime GV, and is derived for adults; bottle-fed infants and small children are recommended an alternative water source if concentrations exceed 3 µg/L, even briefly.

Microcystins are described as the most commonly reported cyanobacterial toxins worldwide, produced mainly by freshwater cyanobacteria with wide geographic/ecological distribution; drinking-water is identified as the most likely human exposure route, with recreational contact during blooms as another potentially high-concentration pathway, and limited data suggesting some food accumulation.

The document attributes high cyanobacterial biomass to nutrient loading from anthropogenic sources (agricultural runoff, wastewater) and, on that basis, recommends nutrient-source control as the primary long-term bloom-management lever.

Conventional drinking-water treatment (coagulation, flocculation, filtration, chlorination) is generally sufficient to bring microcystins to acceptable levels; where it is not, ozonation and activated carbon (filtration or powdered addition) can be effective supplementary steps.

Mechanistically, microcystins are actively imported into cells via organic anion transport proteins (OATPs); because the liver highly expresses OATPs, microcystins act primarily as hepatotoxins, though other OATP-expressing tissues can be affected. Intracellularly they bind protein phosphatases with high affinity, disrupting cytoskeletal, cell-replication, stress-response and DNA-repair pathways, producing acute intrahepatic haemorrhage and, chronically, impaired tumour-proliferation control.

Because the GVs are derived from the single congener MC-LR while environmental exposure is generally to mixtures of MC variants, the document states that comparing total MC concentration (gravimetric or molar) to the GV is 'likely' to be protective in most circumstances — a hedged, not absolute, extrapolation.

The document notes an open scientific disagreement: some recent studies suggest microcystin effects on reproductive organs at lower concentrations than those underlying the liver-based GVs, but these findings are contradicted by earlier studies and are described as requiring corroboration.

Microcystins are cyclic heptapeptides first characterized in the early 1980s (named for Microcystis aeruginosa); more than 250 distinct variants have been identified, with molecular weights of 882–1101 Da; most are hydrophilic and highly water-soluble, with saturation concentrations several orders of magnitude above the WHO guideline values; microcystins and the related nodularins are also described as extremely heat-stable.

title: Cyanobacterial toxins: Microcystins — chemical fact sheet supporting WHO Guidelines for drinking-water quality and safe recreational water environments
year: 2022 (fact sheet compiled as part of WHO Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality, 4th ed. incorporating the 1st and 2nd addenda, per WHO's publications page, confirmed via WebSearch); the in-text guideline-value assessment date for microcystin-LR itself is given as 2003, with an added note that IARC classified microcystin-LR in 2006
what: A WHO chemical fact sheet (Chapter 12 of the Guidelines for Drinking-water Quality) that lays out the health-based provisional drinking-water guideline value, tolerable daily intake, analytical detection limits/methods, monitoring approach, and prevention/treatment options for microcystin-LR, the best-studied cyanobact

claims.c: Microcystins are presented as the best-researched cyanobacterial toxin group and are said to probably occur most frequently in fresh waters among cyanotoxins.

Microcystins are usually cell-bound (intracellular) and are released into surrounding water mainly upon cell rupture/lysis, whereas other cyanotoxins (cylindrospermopsins, saxitoxins, anatoxin-a, anatoxin-a(s)) tend to occur to a larger extent already dissolved — a distinction the source flags as relevant to drinking-water treatment efficacy.

Human exposure to microcystins is largely via drinking-water or recreational contact with cyanobacterial blooms, though microcystins may also occur in fish, molluscs and shellfish from affected water bodies.

More than 80 microcystin variants have been identified, but only a few occur frequently or at high concentration; microcystin-LR is among the most frequent and most toxic congeners and is the only one with a toxicological database sufficient to derive a (provisional) guideline value.
Cyanobacterial genera frequently reported to contain microcystins are Microcystis, Planktothrix and Anabaena.
WHO's provisional drinking-water guideline value for total microcystin-LR (free plus cell-bound) is 0.001 mg/l (1 µg/l).

The guideline value is explicitly labeled provisional because it covers only microcystin-LR, the underlying toxicological database is limited, and new cyanotoxin toxicity data continue to be generated.

The tolerable daily intake (TDI) underpinning the guideline value is 0.04 µg/kg body weight, derived from liver pathology observed in a 13-week oral mouse study, with a 1000-fold uncertainty factor applied because of database gaps, notably the absence of chronic-toxicity and carcinogenicity data.

Three analytical methods for microcystins are compared: HPLC (limit of detection 0.1-1 µg/l), ELISA immunoassay (0.1-0.5 µg/l), and protein phosphatase assay (0.5-1.5 µg/l); HPLC is described as most precise and able to differentiate congeners, while the immunoassay and phosphatase assay are less precise but useful for screening.

WHO's preferred cyanotoxin monitoring approach is visual/microscopic surveillance of source water for rising cyanobacterial cell density or bloom-forming potential, with heightened vigilance once such events occur, rather than routine measurement of finished-water toxin concentration, which the source calls unsatisfactory for safety determination.

Failing to extract the cell-bound (intracellular) toxin fraction during analysis causes a large underestimate of true cyanotoxin concentration, even though this extraction step is described as easy to perform for microcystins.

Mechanistically, microcystin-LR is a potent inhibitor of eukaryotic protein serine/threonine phosphatases 1 and 2A; the liver is the primary toxicological target because microcystins cross cell membranes chiefly via the bile acid transporter.

The guideline-value derivation drew on an oral 13-week mouse study supported by an oral 44-day pig study; the source also notes recorded livestock/wildlife poisonings and published evidence of tumour promotion, and states that in 2006 IARC classified microcystin-LR as a possible human carcinogen (Group 2B).

Blooms are described as occurring when phosphorus and nitrogen concentrations are elevated, particularly in stagnant or very slowly flowing water bodies; blooms recur in the same water bodies, can form surface scums or accumulate at the thermocline of stratified reservoirs, can develop rapidly, and vary in duration from hours to weeks, often on a seasonal pattern.

The most sustainable and effective bloom-prevention measure identified is reducing nutrient loading — particularly phosphorus — into the water body, via control of sewage effluent and catchment sources (erosion, manure, fertilizer); hydrological management such as mixing and flushing can additionally shift the plankton community away from cyanobacteria toward less health-relevant taxa such as diatoms.

For treatment, particle-removal processes (soil/riverbank filtration, flocculation and filtration, dissolved air filtration) are described as effectively controlling the cell-bound microcystin fraction when optimized and operated to avoid cell rupture; free (dissolved) microcystins are said to be well removed by most activated carbon and by chlorination/ozonation at sufficient dose and contact time; potassium permanganate is effective for microcystins specifically (with limited/no data for other cyanotoxins); oxidation is 'not very effective' for saxitoxins; and chlorine dioxide and chloramine are stated to be ineffective for removing cyanotoxins generally.

title: Toxic cyanobacteria in water: a guide to their public health consequences, monitoring and management — Second edition
what: An 859-page, 15-chapter WHO-edited (Chorus & Welker, 2021) technical guide that synthesizes the scientific and practical basis for identifying, assessing, monitoring, and managing public-health risk from cyanobacteria and their toxins in drinking-water and recreational-water settings. It is explicitly the technical bac

claims.c: The 2nd edition (published 28 Feb 2021, ISBN 978-1-003-08144-9, 859 pages) is organized into 15 chapters spanning the full water-use chain — cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin science, exposure, risk assessment, catchment nutrient control, waterbody condition management, drinking-water offtake and treatment, monitoring-programme design, field/laboratory methods, and public health surveillance/communication — and covers four priority cyanotoxin groups: anatoxin-a and analogues, cylindrospermopsins, microcystins, and saxitoxins.
This guide is explicitly the technical background document that WHO used to develop its 2020 drinking-water-quality guidelines and recreational-water-environment guidelines.

A chapter co-authored by Chorus & Zessner (2021) states that hazardous levels of cyanobacterial biomass are unlikely below a total phosphorus (TP) concentration of about 20 µg/L in general water bodies, or about 50 µg/L in shallow water bodies, with the corresponding total-nitrogen (TN) limitation threshold roughly 7 times higher.

A chapter attributed to Welker et al. (2021) identifies remote sensing as one method used to identify and track cyanobacterial blooms, but states this and related approaches still need further development before they are affordable and practical for routine use.

A chapter attributed to Ibelings et al. (2021) argues that climate-change effects on cyanobacterial bloom incidence are more complex than a simple direct-temperature growth-rate effect, because climate change alters hydrophysical conditions and nutrient concentrations in ways that can outweigh the direct temperature effect.

A chapter attributed to Burch, Brookes & Chorus (2021) states that deep, strong water-column mixing can suppress the proliferation of scum-forming cyanobacteria, even though many cyanobacteria otherwise tolerate mixing well.

A chapter attributed to Ibelings et al. (2021) describes a buoyancy-regulation mechanism in which cyanobacteria accumulate carbon (from photosynthesis near the surface) as ballast, causing them to sink and thereby determine their own vertical position in the water column.

A chapter attributed to Padisák et al. (2021) covers microscopy-based estimation of cyanobacterial biomass — quantifying both cell numbers and biovolume — as a laboratory method distinct from chlorophyll-a or phycocyanin pigment-based measurement.

A chapter attributed to Welker et al. (2021) frames monitoring-programme planning as requiring established routines with analytical laboratories, staff training, pilot-phase testing of the sampling scheme, and systematic data storage/documentation.

what: A U.S. EPA summary webpage (part of EPA's Harmful Algal Blooms / HABs program) that presents WHO-developed guidance values for the relative probability of adverse human health effects from recreational (contact/ingestion/inhalation) exposure to cyanobacteria in freshwater, expressed as a three-tier alert framework keye

claims.c: EPA's page summarizes WHO-developed guidance values expressing the relative probability of acute health effects from recreational exposure to cyanobacteria in freshwater, organized as three tiered 'Guidance Levels.'

Guidance Level 1 ('relatively low probability of adverse health effects') is triggered at 20,000 cyanobacterial cells/mL or, alternatively, 10 µg chlorophyll-a/litre; it is derived from a human bathing epidemiological study and is linked to short-term outcomes such as skin irritation and gastrointestinal illness.

Guidance Level 2 ('moderate probability of adverse health effects') is triggered at 100,000 cyanobacterial cells/mL or 50 µg chlorophyll-a/litre; it is derived from WHO's provisional drinking-water guideline value for microcystin-LR together with data on other cyanotoxins, and carries potential for long-term illness with some cyanobacterial species.

Guidance Level 3 ('high probability of adverse health effects') is defined qualitatively by cyanobacterial scum formation in whole-body-contact areas, not by a specific cell-count or chlorophyll-a number, and its basis is inference from oral animal lethal-poisoning data plus documented human poisoning case histories, with acute poisoning risk noted.
The guidance values are scoped specifically to recreational exposure pathways — contact, ingestion, and inhalation — during recreational use of freshwater, not to general environmental exposure.

EPA's page attributes the numeric guidance values to the WHO 2003 publication 'Guidelines for safe recreational water environments. Volume 1, Coastal and fresh waters,' which it describes as based on the WHO 1999 guidelines — i.e., the '1999 Guideline Values' in this source's title are documented on the page via a 2003 WHO publication rather than a document literally titled '1999 guideline values.'

Recommended management actions escalate by guidance level, from posting advisory signage and notifying authorities at Level 1, to watching for scum development at Level 2, up to immediate control of body contact (potentially prohibiting swimming) and a public health investigation at Level 3.

title: Stopping Algae Blooms With Bacteria-Busting Buoys
what: A press release (American Chemical Society "PressPac," syndicated on EurekAlert, phys.org, Mirage News, and Water Online) summarizing a peer-reviewed paper in ACS ES&T Water (DOI 10.1021/acsestwater.5c01257) in which University of Toledo engineers describe and lab-test a passive, "set it and forget it" floating buoy th

claims.c: Researchers built a passive, slow-release algaecide-delivery buoy, explicitly framed as a "set it and forget it" system, intended to be less labor-intensive and cheaper than existing algaecide-application methods that require frequent reapplication.
In laboratory testing with real cyanobacteria-containing Lake Erie water, the buoys eliminated nearly all cyanobacteria within about one week while non-target microbes were largely unaffected.

The buoys come in three sizes (small, medium, large), built from PVC pipe in a "T" or cross shape, with hydrogel disks inserted into the pipe openings to control diffusion of a commercial hydrogen-peroxide-based algaecide into the surrounding water; the buoy visibly tips onto its side when the algaecide is depleted, signaling a refill is needed.
The buoys reliably completed at least four consecutive algaecide-release cycles, each lasting 35 days, before depletion.

Small buoys were lab-tested in approximately 1 liter of cyanobacteria-containing water, monitored over about two weeks, with the water replaced daily using fresh lake water to simulate continuous cyanobacteria exposure.

Beyond beaker-scale lab tests, the research group also ran a pond-scale experiment under ambient/real weather conditions and, per the University of Toledo's own article, was planning a seasonal field deployment with water-treatment operators in northwest Ohio.

The research was funded by the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers; the hydrogen-peroxide-based algaecide used in testing was supplied by SePRO Corporation; University of Toledo researchers Lapitsky, Kober, and Seo have filed a U.S. patent application on the buoy concept, and licensing discussions were underway as of the UToledo News article.
Lead researcher Yakov Lapitsky (Professor and Chair, Department of Chemical Engineering, University of Toledo) frames the practical payoff as conditional on further engineering work.

Co-researcher Youngwoo Seo (Professor, Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, University of Toledo) frames the motivation as shifting bloom control from reactive/labor-intensive manual dosing toward earlier-stage intervention.

The source itself states further research is still needed, specifically to prevent microbial growth ('fouling') on the buoy's surface, and frames the whole concept as requiring successful scale-up before practical deployment; UToledo News separately describes the work as being at an 'early prototype stage' and reports researchers proposing larger buoys to cut the number of units needed for big water bodies from hundreds down to about a dozen.

The press coverage frames the research as motivated by the 2014 Lake Erie cyanobacterial bloom near Toledo, Ohio, which triggered a 'do not drink' water advisory affecting a large regional population.

what: This is the EPA-registered pesticide product label (EPA Reg. No. 67690-55, EPA Est. claims.c: SeClear's active ingredient is copper sulfate pentahydrate at 16.2% of the formulation, equivalent to 4.1% metallic copper, delivering 0.417 lbs of metallic copper per gallon of product.
The product is EPA-registered and labeled for use across a range of aquatic sites, explicitly including potable water sources (subject to restrictions elsewhere in the label).

The product's stated mechanism is rapid disruption of algal cell processes, and it is labeled effective against a broad range of algae growth forms, explicitly including blue-green algae (cyanobacteria), not just green algae.

The label states the product is toxic to fish and aquatic organisms, and that unlike organic pesticides, its copper active ingredient does not degrade and accumulates in the environment with repeated applications.

Treating algae/weeds can deplete dissolved oxygen as biomass decomposes, risking fish and invertebrate suffocation; to mitigate this the label restricts treatment to no more than half a water body at a time with at least 14 days between treatments (with an exception process for public-health emergencies).

Applying the algaecide to high-density cyanobacteria blooms can rupture cells and release intracellular contents into the water, some of which are known mammalian hepatotoxins and neurotoxins; the label advises against treating toxin-producing cyanobacteria at high density for this reason.

Low pH (≤6.5), low dissolved organic carbon (≤3.0 mg/L), and soft water (alkalinity <50 mg/L) are stated to increase acute toxicity of the product to non-target aquatic organisms (e.g., koi, trout); labeled rates assume pH >6.5, DOC >3.0 mg/L, and alkalinity >50 mg/L.

The product does not itself make water potable; for water destined for drinking use, no more than 1.0 ppm metallic copper may be applied in a single treatment, and additional separate potable-water treatment is required.

Table 1 sets application rates by algae growth form: 0.15-1.0 ppm metallic copper (1.0-6.5 gal product/acre-foot) for planktonic and filamentous algae, and 0.4-1.0 ppm (2.5-6.5 gal/acre-foot) for macrophytic algae (Chara/Nitella/Starry Stonewort), with a footnote allowing up to 1.0 ppm for difficult species.

In aquaculture ponds where fish are present, the single-application cap for targeting nuisance algae is lowered to 0.4 ppm metallic copper, versus the general 1.0 ppm single-application maximum.

The label includes a Resistance Management section advising pre- and post-application scouting, listing indicators of suspected resistance, and recommending rotation to a different mechanism of action if resistance is suspected.

title: Harmful Algal Bloom Recreational Health Advisory Guidance
year: 2023 (page states "last updated in 2023"); a related benthic-cyanobacteria supplement is dated 2025
what: A Utah state guidance page (DWQ, with DHHS, last updated 2023, "based largely on guidance from the...EPA") that defines two recreational-exposure advisory tiers — Warning and Danger — for cyanobacteria/cyanotoxin-affected waterbodies. It gives local health departments (LHDs, who hold legal authority to post advisories/

claims.c: The guidance is issued by Utah DWQ in coordination with DHHS, last updated in 2023, and is based largely on EPA guidance updated with newer science on cell-density health impacts.

Legal authority to post recreational health advisories or close a waterbody rests with local health departments (LHDs), not with DWQ/DHHS, which instead provide monitoring, sampling, and interpretation support.

The guidance uses two advisory tiers (Warning, Danger); either tier is triggered if any one of several independent indicators (cyanobacteria cell density or any of four cyanotoxins) is exceeded, and Danger can also be triggered directly by a plausible human/animal illness report regardless of lab results.

Warning Advisory (recommendation to avoid primary contact recreation such as swimming, waterskiing, wading) is tied to specific lab thresholds: cyanobacteria cell density, and concentrations of microcystins, cylindrospermopsin, anatoxin-a, and saxitoxin.

Danger Advisory (recommendation to stay away from the waterbody entirely, reflecting risk of short-term effects, long-term illness, or death) uses substantially higher lab thresholds for three of the four toxins: microcystins, anatoxin-a, and saxitoxin.

A separate, lower set of numeric values (cyanobacteria >20,000 cells/mL; microcystin >4 µg/L; cylindrospermopsin >4 µg/L; anatoxin-a "detected at any level") also appears on the page, described by the fetch tool as coming from a decision flowchart/diagram distinct from the main thresholds table.

An advisory is not lifted until two consecutive water samples, collected at least one week apart, both show cyanotoxin/cell-density levels back below the relevant threshold -- i.e., a minimum multi-week confirmation window.

As of 2025, Utah issued a separate benthic-cyanobacteria ("harmful algae mat") advisory guidance because the existing planktonic-bloom HAB guidance and its cell-density/toxin thresholds do not apply to mat-forming benthic cyanobacteria; the new guidance instead uses percent areal mat coverage as its primary metric.

Advisory determinations draw on visual bloom confirmation plus laboratory water sampling for cell density and the four named cyanotoxins, supplemented by documented Standard Operating Procedures for phytoplankton sample collection, SPATT (Solid Phase Adsorption Toxin Testing) passive toxin samplers, and fish/wildlife carcass sampling during mortality events.

The page's own descriptive text refers to "four independent indicators" underlying the advisory decision, but enumerates cell density, microcystin, cylindrospermopsin, and anatoxin-a in that description while saxitoxin thresholds are also given in the tables -- an internal count/labeling inconsistency (four named vs. five thresholds actually tabulated) that this dossier flags rather than resolves.

title: Public Water System Harmful Algal Bloom Response Strategy
what: An October 2025 Ohio EPA (Division of Drinking and Ground Waters) regulatory guidance document that sets numerical cyanotoxin "do-not-drink" thresholds for finished drinking water and defines HAB/cyanotoxin monitoring, sampling, and analytical-method requirements for surface-water public water systems (PWSs) in Ohio. I

claims.c: For children under 6 (including bottle-fed infants) and other sensitive populations, Ohio's finished-drinking-water 'Do Not Drink' thresholds are 0.3 μg/L microcystins, 0.3 μg/L anatoxin-a, 0.7 μg/L cylindrospermopsin, and 0.3 μg/L saxitoxins.
For children 6 and older and adults, the corresponding thresholds are higher: 1.6 μg/L microcystins, 1.6 μg/L anatoxin-a, 3.0 μg/L cylindrospermopsin, and 1.6 μg/L saxitoxins.

The microcystins and saxitoxins thresholds are applied to the total of all reported congeners/variants (not a single congener reading), and Ohio EPA additionally applies established saxitoxin toxicity-equivalency factors when judging whether a threshold is exceeded.
The child and adult drinking-water thresholds for all four cyanotoxins are unchanged from Ohio's prior (2022) version of this strategy.

Ohio's 'sensitive populations' category (held to the children-under-6 threshold tier for all four cyanotoxins) includes pregnant women, nursing mothers, and dialysis patients; people with pre-existing liver conditions are added to that category only for microcystins and cylindrospermopsin, because those two toxins are hepatotoxins, while elderly and immunocompromised individuals receive only a non-binding recommendation to consider following the children's guidance.

Ohio's regulatory definition of a HAB is either visible water discoloration from cyanobacteria, or a cyanobacteria cell count above 4,000 cells/mL for genera capable of producing cyanotoxins, citing Shambaugh and Brines (2003).
A public water system must notify Ohio EPA of any finished-water cyanotoxin detection within 24 hours, using a dedicated after-hours spill hotline if needed.

For saxitoxins, cylindrospermopsin, and anatoxin-a specifically (unlike microcystins, which has its own dedicated notification rule, OAC 3745-90-06), Ohio EPA's 24-hour public-notification timeline after a confirmed resample/repeat exceedance is a recommendation rather than a codified rule requirement, with Ohio EPA evaluating results case-by-case with the PWS.
Among the four regulated cyanotoxins, microcystins and saxitoxins are described as the most commonly detected in Ohio waters, while cylindrospermopsin and anatoxin-a are detected far less often.

The document frames its precautionary posture around the fact that no antidote exists for cyanotoxins, and notes that Ohio's health and agriculture departments have received reports of probable human illness and dog deaths from cyanotoxin exposure, with the CDC's OHHABS system tracking cases nationwide.

Beyond direct toxicity, cyanobacteria increase organic material in source water, which drives production of disinfection byproducts described as carcinogenic, plus taste-and-odor compounds that affect drinking-water palatability.

The document's background section associates cyanobacterial blooms with eutrophic/hypereutrophic conditions (excess nitrogen and phosphorus) and states bloom formation is favored by nutrient availability, sufficient light, and warmer temperatures, with spatial distribution shaped by weather, hydrology, morphology, currents, and wind.

Every public water system with a surface-water source in Ohio must maintain a Treatment Optimization Protocol (TOP) describing how it will optimize its existing treatment specifically for microcystins removal.

title: State of Ohio Harmful Algal Bloom Response Strategy for Recreational Waters
what: A 2020 State of Ohio interagency policy and operations document (Ohio EPA, Ohio Department of Natural Resources, and Ohio Department of Health) that lays out a unified statewide strategy for detecting, reporting on, and posting public-health advisories for cyanobacterial harmful algal blooms (HABs) at Ohio's recreation

claims.c: The Strategy's stated purpose is to give Ohio a unified, statewide, multi-agency approach to addressing HABs in recreational waters and to protect people from cyanotoxins, operationalized through numeric thresholds used to make advisory decisions.

Ohio deliberately concentrates HAB sampling and advisory effort on public beaches rather than open-water recreation (boating, jet skiing), reasoning that beach-goers face substantially greater cyanotoxin exposure potential (full-body immersion, more children) than open-water users, who instead get caution signage and outreach.

Responsibility for observing/sampling blooms and posting advisories is divided by water-body type and ownership: ODNR is primary contact for state-park beaches; local beach managers/local health districts handle other public beaches; property owners are responsible for private beaches; USACE coordinates with ODNR on jointly managed lakes; and Ohio River responsibilities split between ORSANCO, local government, and Ohio EPA depending on access type. ODH separately runs illness surveillance via the One Health Harmful Algal Bloom System (OHHABS) and hosts the public BeachGuard advisory-posting website.

The document frames cyanobacterial blooms as generally occurring in eutrophic/hypereutrophic water bodies, and states (citing external literature) that eutrophication is most often the result of elevated nitrogen and phosphorus supply enhancing phytoplankton/aquatic-plant production.

Among cyanotoxins the state screens for, microcystins and saxitoxin are described as the more commonly detected in Ohio waters, while cylindrospermopsin and anatoxin-a are detected much less frequently.

Cyanotoxins are colorless, odorless, and tasteless, can persist in the water after a visible bloom has disappeared, and toxin production is strain-specific (a given cyanobacterium may produce one, several, or no toxin types) — meaning visible bloom presence does not reliably indicate toxin presence or absence.

Because cyanotoxins are potent and no antidote is known, Ohio explicitly adopted a conservative (health-protective) policy stance when setting its recreational-water thresholds, rather than thresholds calibrated to a precise toxicological safety margin.

Ohio's Recreational Public Health Advisory thresholds are 8 µg/L for microcystins, 8 µg/L for anatoxin-a, 15 µg/L for cylindrospermopsin, and 0.8 µg/L for saxitoxins, with the microcystins and saxitoxin values applied to the total of all reported congeners/variants/analogs.

The microcystins and cylindrospermopsin thresholds in this document are not Ohio-original values — they were issued by U.S. EPA as final recommended recreational-swimming advisories in June 2019 and were then formally adopted by Ohio.

The stated thresholds are described as protective of human health via incidental ingestion based on current information, but the source explicitly states they may not be protective of animals such as dogs or livestock.

ODH has built a clinician-facing diagnostic aid called 'Screen for Green,' a flowchart to help physicians assess a patient's probable cyanotoxin exposure and treatment options, with an analogous resource for veterinarians.

The state uses NOAA/NASA-processed satellite remote-sensing imagery (under the multi-agency Cyanobacteria Assessment Network, CyAN) to help locate HABs in Lake Erie, inland state-park lakes, and the Ohio River, but the source frames this imagery's role explicitly as a human visual-confirmation aid for recreation managers, not an automated or stand-alone detection/forecasting output.

The state's satellite remote-sensing tools are stated to work only on lakes/rivers at least 300 meters wide, and can detect blooms approximately 1-2 feet below the surface (beyond human visual detection) and in turbid water where visual identification is difficult.

NOAA has produced a bi-weekly Lake Erie satellite bulletin (bloom imagery, predicted densities, wind directions) for some time, but a comparable inland-lake satellite product for Ohio has only existed since May 2017, reviewed near-daily by Ohio EPA.

The source explicitly warns that the absence of a visible surface bloom does not mean a bloom is absent, since blooms can move to another part of a lake or mix through the water column instead of dying off, and some cyanobacteria never form surface scums at all.

Color is explicitly described as an unreliable way to distinguish cyanobacteria from green algae or suspended sediment; the source states satellite imagery, microscopic examination, or dedicated screening tools (molecular methods, cyanotoxin field test kits) are needed to confirm cyanobacteria presence.

The advisory thresholds and overall guidelines are not presented as fixed/permanent: they were set by an interagency committee, are reviewed and updated annually, supersede prior versions, and are explicitly stated as likely to require future revision as HAB toxicity science evolves.
